per_turn_leaks.student_message: And when these episodes occur, what does your body do?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: When the chest tightness hits, I get pretty sweaty and feel a bit nauseous, but no vomiting. It's like my whole body is just on edge, you know? The sweating and nausea are usually pretty bad during the more severe episodes, but it's not always consistent.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Let's focus on the episodes. Walk me through one from start to finish.
per_turn_leaks.patient_response:
It usually starts with a tightness in my chest that I can feel when I'm driving or if I've been lifting something heavy. It's hard to describe, but it feels like someone is squeezing my chest really tightly. Sometimes it's mild and I can just shake it off, but other times it gets worse and I start to feel short of breath. That's when I know I need to stop what I'm doing and take a break.

As the tightness worsens, I get this burning sensation in my lungs and it feels like I'm not getting enough air. It's scary because I've had episodes where I was driving on the highway and suddenly I couldn't catch my breath. That's when I pull over as soon as I can and try to calm down.

The sweating and nausea are usually pretty bad during these episodes, but it's not always consistent. Sometimes I'll get really sweaty and feel like I'm going to be sick, while other times it's just a mild tingling sensation in my chest. But the shortness of breath is always there – it's what really worries me.

It can last anywhere from 10 minutes to an hour, depending on how severe it is. Sometimes I'll take some deep breaths and try to calm down, and it will go away on its own. Other times, I'll need to sit back and relax for a bit before I feel better.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: During an episode, do you notice anything besides the main symptom?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: You know, like I said earlier, during the more severe episodes, I get this tingling sensation in my left arm. It's not always there, but when it is, it can be a bit unsettling. It's like a numbness or a pins-and-needles feeling, and it usually goes away once the episode passes. But yeah, that's something else I've noticed during those intense episodes.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Specifically, any changes in your skin — like sweating or flushing?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: Yeah, as I mentioned earlier, I do get pretty sweaty during these episodes. The sweating is usually one of the first things I notice when the chest tightness hits. It's not just a little bit either – it can be really excessive. And sometimes, my face might feel warm or flushed, especially if I'm feeling anxious about what's happening. But sweating is definitely the more noticeable symptom for me.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: And your stomach — any queasiness or nausea during these moments?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: I already mentioned that I get a bit nauseous during the episodes, but no vomiting. It's usually pretty mild, just a feeling like my stomach is churning or something. But yeah, nausea is definitely one of those associated symptoms for me.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: What about other parts of your body? Any pain, tingling, or unusual sensations?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: You know, besides the chest tightness and shortness of breath, I've also noticed some mild bilateral ankle swelling lately. My shoes feel a bit tighter than usual, but it's not too bad. And to be honest, I haven't really done anything about it because I've been so focused on dealing with this chest issue.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Now let's zoom out. Any swelling you've noticed anywhere on your body?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: Yeah, like I said, I've had some mild bilateral ankle swelling lately. My shoes feel tighter than usual, but it's not too bad. That's about it, though.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Now family history. Any medical problems run in your family?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response: My mother has hypertension and type 2 diabetes, and she's still alive at 78 years old, which is a good sign for me, I suppose. My father had a heart attack at age 52 and passed away, but that was before my own health issues started to pop up.

per_turn_leaks.student_message: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious or chronic conditions?
per_turn_leaks.patient_response:
My mother's hypertension and diabetes are a concern for me, especially since I've been diagnosed with those same conditions. But it's good to know that she's still doing well at 78. As for my father, his heart attack was pretty young – he had one at 52 and didn't make it. That does give me some cause for worry, but like I said, I'm trying to focus on managing my own health right now.

I don't have any siblings, so it's just the two of us with our parents' medical history. But my wife has been really supportive throughout all this, and we're working together to try and get my health back on track.
